questions.prompt: Your team noticed cache hit rate dropped from 65% to 28% over a week. What's the impact and where do you investigate?

choices.text: No big deal; users don't notice cache hit rate.

Origin traffic just doubled (cache misses up); latency on uncached requests adds up; origin load and DB load increased. Investigate: recent changes that might have added varying headers (cookies, query params, dynamic timestamps), cache-control headers, or invalidation patterns. Weekly dashboard review catches this kind of drift early.

questions.explanation: Cache hit rate is a high-leverage operational metric. Drops mean origin sees more traffic, latency rises, and bills can grow. Common causes: vary-by-cookie / vary-by-query changes, cache-control header drift, missing edge cache rules. Catch in weekly review before it becomes an incident.

questions.prompt: Where do Cloudflare cost surprises typically come from?

choices.text: Viral Workers traffic blowing through included tiers, R2 Class A/B operations at scale, D1 per-row read pricing under heavy reads, Workers AI inference scaling beyond expectations, Logpush destinations (Datadog/Splunk) charging by ingest. Set per-product alerts and review weekly.

questions.explanation: DDoS protection is free. Cost surprises usually live in usage-based items: Workers requests/CPU, R2 ops, D1 reads, Workers AI. Set alerts when daily/weekly usage exceeds expected; review monthly bills against projections.
